title: 2026/04/02 - JU [Part 3]
displayTitle: 2026-04-02 - Justice Committee - Inquiry into the 2025 Local Elections [Part 3]
description: Thursday, 2 April 2026 | Justice Committee Inquiry into the 2025 Local Elections Hearing of Evidence Submitters: Transparency International New Zealand Inc Julie Haggie, Executive Director Green Party Local Government Caucus Alan Somerville, Co-convenor Christchurch City Council Jo Daly, Council Governance Advisor / Deputy Electoral Office Auckland Council Councillor Richard Hills, Chair of Policy, Planning & Development Committee Elodie Fontaine Kay Jones Charlotte Melser It's in the Ballot Productions Sam Somers, Co-Chair, It's in the Ballot Productions Nick Holander, Candidate Liaison Jette de Jong Election Services Dale Ofsoske, Managing Director and Principal Electoral Officer Craig Taylor, Business Manager and Electoral Officer
descriptionSummary: Inquiry into the 2025 Local Elections. Transparency International New Zealand Inc. Julie Haggie, Executive Director

chunks.text: Behind now, are we? Yeah. We are. Sorry. Sorry. Hello.
Transparency International. They're in by Zoom.

Hi there. Oh, hello. How are you? Yeah, sorry about that. I just- No, that's cool... tried to sort this out. We've got 10 minutes with you. The committee, just for people beaming in for this, please leave some time for questions, otherwise I'll just have to cut you off, and that's not very helpful. The committee does like to ask questions.

We've all read your submissions, so we don't have to keep going through belaboring them because we've read them. Okay. The time is yours. Thanks. Sure. I think I'm taking that the submission is read. I just think there's three main issues, and that was one is the integrity of electoral processes generally. The second is oversight and accountability and reporting, especially on things like special votes.

We don't think there's anybody who's got an eye on that, and I'm afraid it doesn't seem to be the Select Committee either. The third one is education and information for new candidates, and then there's misinformation as well and disinformation. But those other ones are the main ones. So what we think is, and I think other people have spoken on it, is that the kind of rigor that's enabled by the Electoral Commission running the central election is not then enabled in the local government elections, and that's putting

stress on everywhere. We've recommended a move away from postal voting, because that's clearly not working, and appreciate there's a lot of complexity in there. But also, we're really concerned about recent events in the last election. Realize that none have got to the police prosecution yet, but they were certainly significant enough to void an election result.

And that was of serious concern, probably the largest. Whatever it was, somebody took those votes out of people's letterboxes. Somebody faked the votes, and that's a hugely serious issue that needs to be dealt with more than just by the police. So I would've thought an inquiry would've been the best approach to that. Anyway, I think that's all I'm saying.

We've got sort of what we've said, and so I'm happy to answer questions. All right. Thank you very much. I've got first question from Mr. Carl Bates. Thanks very much, Chair. Thanks very much for your submission. Found it really an interesting read. Was interested in your point made under 2B1 about conduct of elections and processes prior to election day for raising issues, and just interested if you had any thoughts about how that could occur.

Yes. I've been trying to nut that through as well. How would you actually manage it? I suppose you could have the initiation of an inquiry at the point at which it's raised. It's possible that I don't think there was any ability for the electoral officer to have initial inquiries, to have an inquiry and query.

It certainly is a problem, and it's not for me to work out because I think it needs a good legal brain to work out what's fair and due process around that. I think that's true. After all, you are in the middle of an election. You don't want to attract people attacking others just for the sake of putting an election off. You don't want to besmirch the name of any. But on the other hand, it was such a serious issue and it could only be dealt with afterwards, and I think there has to be a mechanism.

If there was an Electoral Commission role, that would be the place for it to be considered. So it would be lodged with the Electoral Commission during the election process. Okay. Okay. Ms. Celia Boyd. Celia Wade-Brown, sorry. Thank you. Kia ora, thank you very much for your submission and your concerns about transparency and democracy.

You've mentioned the report of the local government electoral working group, and you would generally believe that having local elections run by the Electoral Commission would be more transparent than by two private companies that we can't use the Official Informations Act on.

That's correct. There's a number of reasons why we think it would be better, and that education element also goes to that. And also transparency. And we tried very hard to find what people were being told at different sites. Seems to be quite variable. There does not seem to be any consideration in many of them, that they're not actually talking about what you cannot do and what's ethically not able to be done or legally not able to be done.

Trying to pressure voters, for example, should be something that's 101 really in a candidate's briefing. That's the problem with a lot of new candidates being new on every local body election. You've got the potential for many more people to really not understand what the electoral process is about, and I think that was seen in a couple of other examples there.

Yeah. Thank you. All right. Hey, got to thank you for your submission. It was a fascinating submission, particularly going through all that analysis, so really appreciate you bringing that to our attention. Good. I do think the problem with special votes is also it's still not available on the Department of Internal Affairs website.

So that kind of lack of transparency about what's happening with special votes in those local elections is a real problem, and it means that you're not able to do your job properly either. So I think that's a significant issue around the elections, and that's partly because you've got private providers doing that work. Okay. Thank you. Right. Now we've got the Green Party via Zoom.
Mr Summerville, are you with us? He's on board? Just coming in. Just coming through. Okay. Mr Summerville, if you're there, that'd be great.

Good morning, Mr Summerville. Good morning. Now we've got 10 minutes with you. Right. Thank you very much. Tēnā koutou katoa. Ka mihi mahana kia koutou. I'm Alan Summerville. I'm co-convener of the Greens local government caucus. Our local government caucus represents 28 elected members around the country who were endorsed by the Green Party in the 2025 elections.

I'm a regional councilor with the Otago Regional Council, and I'm here in Ōtepoti, Dunedin. I'll concentrate on a few parts of our written submission, the parts where I have the closest personal knowledge and experience. I guess our starting point is the belief that it should be straightforward for people to vote, and that goes back into the principles. I can refer to the principles of the Local Electoral Act 2001, where it says, "All qualified persons have a reasonable and equal opportunity to cast an informed vote." And

it talks about public confidence and public understanding of local electoral processes. So, I think my first point is that the postal voting system is well past its use-by date, and it effectively excludes many people from casting a vote. I'll relate some of my experience from Dunedin last year.

In July last year, Green Party members did a round of door knocking in different parts of Dunedin with the intent of asking people, "Are you enrolled?" And getting them enrolled to vote. One area we concentrated on was North Dunedin, an area with a high transient population, with many tertiary students there. What we found was mail composting in letterboxes down the street- Right... including piles of orange Electoral Commission envelopes. And naturally, of course, we directed people to the Vote NZ website to get them enrolled.

We returned to the area in September and found in North Dunedin, the majority of people had not received their voting papers. That was either because they had moved flats from last year and not updated their address in spite of our best efforts, or they were enrolled at their parent's address in another town, and so did not receive their voting papers. So the postal voting system, it's certainly not the whole explanation for the low voter turnout, but I think it is part of it.

As a solution, I'd go with the LGNZ proposals. I think you've heard from them this morning, and they've gone into that in great detail. So please, let's retire the postal voting system and have something better in place before 2028. Another important point for us is to say let's use STV for all elections and all councils.

Be consistent and use the more democratic STV approach. Again, using Otago for an example, the Otago Regional Council and the Dunedin City Council use STV. The other councils in the region use first past the post. So, it ended up with the situation that voters in Queenstown Lakes District Council, Waitaki District Council, Central Otago, and Clutha had two voting systems to contend with in the same set of voting papers.

I see there's evidence presented to you in the submission by the Greater Wellington Regional Council saying- ... there's robust data across numerous triennial elections, clear evidence of a higher level of informal votes where electors are asked to apply different voting systems in multi-member elections. So, let's not put those additional barriers for people.

Let's use the one system. Also propose to increase government resourcing to aid the participation of people with disabilities. One specific suggestion from us is to apply the Electoral Access Fund also to local body elections. So going back to the Local Electoral Act 2001, all qualified persons have a reasonable and equal opportunity to accept nomination as a candidate, and that use of the Electoral Access Fund would assist with that.

You'll have seen that a part of our submission was on handling of roll data. There are some specific recommendations in there. That's not my area of expertise. Mm. But that's still useful stuff, and I'll be pleased to take any questions now. Right. Well, rather unsurprisingly, we've got Celia Wade-Brown who wants to ask you a question.

Kia ora, Alan. Thank you for presenting this. I'm interested in the area of consistency between local and central government election processes. Yep. And, the postal versus in-person, and the commission running the education makes sense. Presumably, you would want to see some alignment in the enrollment rules.

So at the moment, as I understand it, the local government you can enroll up to the day before. Until recently, central government you could enroll on the day, and now that's been rolled back. Should we settle on something that is common to both systems? I think that consistency is very helpful, and in that example that you have given, our very strong preference would be to be able to enroll right up until election day. But yeah, there are a number of places where that consistency can be brought in, and one way of achieving that

would be to have the Electoral Commission managing local body, local council elections as well. Thank you. Can I ask you a question? You said in respect of overseas voters, they should get their voting papers earlier. Yeah. How many people actually vote, if they're living in London or New York or Singapore, in a local election in little old New Zealand? I can imagine we have difficulty even for a national election.

Do you know what the numbers are? Look, I'm afraid I do not know the answer to that, no. Sorry about that. I know, a specific question. Anyway, Pete, I don't think I've got any other questions. Thank you for your time this morning. It's a good submission. Thanks for the opportunity to present. Kia ora. Right. Now we're going to Jo Daly from the Christchurch City Council by Zoom.

Are you there? Oh, good morning. Just, yeah, we're still going. Now, we've got 10 minutes with you. We have read your submission, but the time is yours. Tena koutou. I'm Jo Daly. I'm the council's governance advisor, and I'm also our deputy electoral officer.

I do have an apology from our elected members who are currently sitting through annual plan hearings, day two of many. Oh. You've obviously got the council's submission, and I'll just reiterate a few key points from that, including those related to your terms of reference. And I'll also just introduce a couple of points from some work council staff have done post-election, in analyzing the voter turnout and from some survey responses to some election-related questions.

Council's consistently submitted in support of changes to legislation to allow for the modernization of voting, increasing civic education awareness nationally, accessibility, including to those overseas, and improving central government support and funding for local elections.

Like probably everyone you're hearing from, we are concerned at the decline in voter participation and the ability to attract good candidates, and these matters are in line with the national trends. The Justice Committee, in the time I've been working closely with elections, has made many recommendations relating to these matters, which disappointingly haven't been progressed.

In particular, the consistent theme from those recommendations is regarding modernization and alternative voting methods, review of postal challenges, including alternatives for overseas, and the review of voting systems and methods. So we're really keen to see some of those recommendations and recommendations you yourselves may make actually progressed, rather than staying in a report.

Just regarding the voting process, the key question you asked regarding disallowed special voters. We had 82% of those that returned special voting documents allowed, and the majority of those disallowed were because they weren't able to be verified on the electoral roll. So that could have rolled into the next point regarding the electoral rolls. We didn't have any significant issues with them.

But the majority of electors who enrolled were on the unpublished roll. Who enrolled, sorry, past the close the final roll, didn't receive clear or full information on what they needed to do to be able to vote. So we see that that is really an opportunity for change, particularly those unpublished roll electors.

We've got over 3,000 in Christchurch, and only 26% of them requested special voting documents, because they receive correspondence from the Electoral Commission, but there's not much detail in there apart from contact the council if you want to make a vote.

There's an opportunity to put more information in there. I'll be strongly supportive of that. Just regarding the vote bins, we had 64 orange vote bins across Christchurch, in all our council facilities, including our recreation centers. We also had them at the airport, at the university library, and in supermarkets. So, they worked really well, and neighboring councils used similar and we were promoting that voting documents could be returned across those boundaries because we all use the same election service provider.

And we also had special voting and on-site voting available from 12 sites across the city. So that was Monday to Friday, and we also had sites open specifically for that on the weekend prior to election day and election day. So there's quite a lot of effort made in making it as accessible as possible.

We didn't have any significant issues raised regarding integrity in Christchurch, or not that I or our electoral officer were aware of. But reports from candidates about online activity from other candidates and the public, which I'm sure you're well aware of, those kind of concerns.

We did receive a number of queries regarding the requirements, entitlements, or qualifications necessary for a person to stand as an elected member, which was something very-We have an external electoral officer now, and that has worked well with our staff. And with regard to the Electoral Commission, as you'll see from the submission, strongly support an increased role in them with good consultation on how that works.

Myself and our communications team work really closely with our local Electoral Commission engagement team. We attend events together. We make sure there's always voting information on our sites and things like that, and I think that's quite a great partnership and a really good example of what perhaps could happen nationally. And just a couple of comments on our participation that you would've seen.

We had reduced participation down to 38%. We've got 16 geographical wards across the city, and the turnout varied from 53% in Banks Peninsula, a smaller community, to 27% in Riccarton. We sourced some information from the Electoral Commission to match with data provided by our service provider on those that voted.

The results weren't surprising. Only one in four electors from 18 to 24 voted. Over half the electors from 65 to 79 voted. Demographics covered, 25% of electors with Maori descent voted, compared with 39% without. And many of the wards with that lower turnout were also wards with higher instances of socioeconomic deprivation.

And we had our Life in Christchurch survey, which is an annual survey we do. It went out to 40,000 people, and we had nearly 4,500 surveys returned. And these are people that have engaged with council or put themselves on this panel. But some of the key statistics from that report on responses, which we're still finalizing, is 90% of those people who responded indicated they voted. Eighty-nine percent of those respondents indicated they found it very easy or easy to vote. And the biggest identified reason for not voting

was they didn't get round to it, they couldn't find enough information about the candidates, or they couldn't find a candidate that appealed. And the final comments I'll make, that they told us, were what would make it easier to vote. Over half the respondents indicated being able to vote online. As you'd imagine, we had a lot of comments about postal being out of date, unhelpful, not user-friendly.

We had 42% of those respondents wanted more information about candidates and what they stand for, and 28% of those candidates want... Of those respondents, sorry, want candidates to be more active in the community during the election. Mm-hmm. So thank you for the opportunity to proceed.

Questions if there's time? Yes. We've got one from Mr. Dan Roseborne. Thanks, Joe. You mention here about voting documents seeing 34% being delivered by New Zealand Post. And I'm just wondering with, with New Zealand Post winding back a lot of the services across Christchurch and Canterbury, how that might affect, well, the next election if we continue with the status quo with our voting system?

Well, we used a mixture of New Zealand Post and DX. I'm not aware of any significant issues with that delivery, but obviously a reduction in services raises concerns about the viability of post, and just referring to the comments from the previous submitter, regarding use of letterboxes these days and how many people actually do get posted mail.

Can I, are you right there, Dan? Can I just ask, you obviously seem to run a pretty good operation. What are your institutional arrangements? Some councils use third parties. Is yours basically in-house, the promotion, all that sort of stuff? Is that what I understood from or implied from your comments before?

We've got an external electoral officer and an external election service provider. But promotion and sort of the general operations, excluding the processing of votes and distribution of votes, is done by council- Okay... or the special vote allocation.

All right. Thank you. I've got Celia Wade-Brown. Thank you, Joe. I do have a question about how, presumably you support the local government New Zealand Electoral Working Group thing that wants Electoral Commission to take on a bigger role. How do you see that best work... If we were to go down that route at some stage, how do we combine that national reach with a more local focus on who the candidates are, where the meetings are, that side of getting people to really think local about who they're voting for?

Just acknowledging I'm presenting the council's submission, so I may have some more detailed views. But in short, in our submission, we've referenced the need for consultation on how that's going to work and potentially that overarching national messaging and things like that with local promotion being led by the councils. Whether or not I would see that should be a partnership with the commission. But sort of aspects I think would need council leadership.

Thank you. Okay. Thank you very much for your contribution this morning and joining us from Christchurch. Okay. Thank you for the opportunity. Right. Now we've got Councillor Richard Hills from Auckland Council by Zoom. Hopefully, I think I've seen him on there. Mr. Hills, are you there?

Good morning. We've got 10 minutes with you. We've read your submission, so leave a little bit of time for questions, please. Tēnā koe, Chair. Thank you very much, and kia ora koutou katoa to the committee. I'll read a lot of what I'm going to say just to make sure I get it right. But yes, you've read our submission.

But largely, obviously, the 29% turnout for Auckland, in my opinion, disaster for democracy. But it is very concerning for us. We do believe there are a range of factors that have led to that decline of participation, but we do consider that postal voting is part of that. I think it was around 38% of the people who said they didn't vote, saying the reason was they filled it out, they didn't take it back, they forgot to. And that's quite a big section of the community who just didn't pop it back. So we think that postal voting and the

issues there. It also extended the timeframe this year because of the postal service situation. We had to have an additional week or so because of the time that letters go out and letters can come back in. We consider that greater national leadership and coordination of the administration of local elections is required.

In particular, the council reiterates its support for the Electoral Commission to take responsibility for delivering local elections, given its experience administering parliamentary elections and the operational capability and independence required to manage the elections at scale. Council would welcome strengthening the integrity framework surrounding elections.

Current processes rely heavily on the unsuccessful candidates initiating court proceedings to challenge election results, and Council recommends that you could introduce automatic recounts where margins are small, such we had some margins that never got to a recount as low as seven votes, than that.

Hmm. We acknowledge that voting method reform alone will not reverse declining participation. A broad and nationally coordinated approach is required to improve public understanding of local government, encourage participation in local elections, include strengthening civic education, increasing visibility of local elections to a level comparable to parliamentary elections, and consider structural reforms to enable greater alignment between local and central functions.

We obviously recommend the aim is to modernize the local electoral system, strengthen electoral integrity, and support renewed public participation. We do think we need to transition away from postal, even if the government removed postal as an option. It just has become fairly untenable when, as a candidate delivering pamphlets to many letterboxes, seeing just piles of them sitting in the mud, wet, and we have had issues up here, as you know, with the whole election being quashed due to some concerns around the integrity of those

ballots and whether people were collecting ballots to vote for others. The advance voting issues, when we had 188, I think, boxes around the whole city, we had good access to drop votes off if people had them, and we did spend a lot of money on advertising as a council. It's the private organization that does the voting side of things. We recommend that voters who are overseas during the election period are enabled to access and return their voting documents like the parliamentary elections.

So parliamentary elections are really easy. If you're overseas, you can basically take a picture of your vote and send it in. That is not what is enabled for local government. Special votes, we want that to be reviewed to simplify the information required. About, I don't think it was half, about 40% of our special votes, several thousand, were disallowed, and some of that was to do with occupation being incorrect or occupation not being written.

That is not a requirement in parliamentary special votes. Mm-hmm. Required in local government. So if someone was a student or someone changed their job or someone just didn't want to fill out the either butcher or baker or a candlestick maker, that was actually their vote was disallowed. And when you had areas where there was a change, someone not getting elected by seven or eight votes, that could have been the difference.

We want to continue the provision of telephone dictation voting, more support for voters with disabilities. We already talked about recounts and safeguards. Sorry. All right. We've got a few questions. So- We also... Oh, sorry, the last one was just we support extending local government to four years if the government does, potentially having them at the same time. The other one was investigating compulsory voting.

In Brisbane, I think they had a turnout for local elections of 85%. They have compulsory voting, whereas in Western Australia they don't, and they're down about the same as us, 30%. So just investigating that for local government. Right. First question is from Rima Nakde. Hi, Richard. Kia ora. In your introduction right now, you've been talking about, we did this, we found this. Who is we?

The Auckland Council, sorry. Okay. So there's 19 other councilors. Which other councilors were you in liaison with, with respect to this report? The submission has been approved by all councilors and had local board input as well. Uh- This is not my personal submission, sorry.

But did you actually talk to them and see what can you add to this report, to this submission? Everyone. We had workshops, we had abilities to put information in, and then it was debated at full council Okay, awesome. Thank you. Can I ask another question? Yeah. Thank you. With respect to, you said the integrity of ballot papers.

Yes. What are you referring to, Councillor? So there was lots of anecdotal information of people seeing, including myself, ballots strewn across neighborhoods, sitting wet in areas. Maurice Williamson talked about them being in letterboxes of houses that were removed for the eastern busway that didn't exist at all.

And then we have had a whole election quashed through a process that the courts decided that there were so many votes of people that were voted for, but those people said they didn't vote. So there were a lot of ballots that were- Which one was that? Just to be absolutely clear.

Just to be absolutely- It's the Ōtara-Papatōetoe's- Yeah... election. So at the moment, the election's going on at the moment. So- Yeah... there clearly is an issue there, but also everyone has a story about seeing just tons of votes sitting, and people going, "Oh"- Yeah, I know. We've got the point.

Right. Yeah. I've got a question from Camelia Bilic. Thank you. Camilla Bilic. Camilla, sorry. Thank you. Thanks, Richard, for your submission. I think the point that you raised around the issue with special votes is something that the committee should note, and I hope that we're able to address that in our report. The question I had for you, though, is, you may or may not have had the opportunity to see the report of local government New Zealand, which is obviously separate from council now. I just wanted to know if you agreed with,

or Auckland Council agreed with their recommendations. And specifically, there seemed to be quite a bit of alignment between what you were suggesting with the Electoral Commission. We also asked them about how that would be paid for, and they indicated that it would be maybe a proposed suggestion was the central government and local government paying some of the costs with there being a levying structure. Has Auckland Council body thought into how the Electoral Commission change, if that was implemented, might be funded?

Yeah, I guess there are already costs that would be shared anyway. There's already Electoral Commission already doing this work. So if we were able to tap into that, the same people, the same trained people, to be able to spread that, and maybe that would be good training for the central government election as well. Not training, because both should be important.

But, at the moment, we do our own structures, pay our own way, have a private organization do the voting. If throughout the process that that was shared, it would probably be cheaper, I imagine, over time for everyone. But also it's standardized for everyone. They've got one port of call for both elections. Thank you. That's all? Right. Celia Wade-Brown.

Thank you. Good morning, Richard. I had a question on the special votes, not the disallowed ones, I think that's been covered well. But that very big increase in the number of special votes. And I wondered two things about it. First of all, do you think that the new ability for enrollment details to be automatically updated, like if somebody changes their address through Waka Kotahi, would help?

Secondly, do you know if people tried to enroll or update their enrollment details on the morning? Because local government, you have to have enrolled by the day before. Do you have any information on that? There were people saying that they couldn't enroll on the day, but you could enroll right up to the day, which is helpful, but it's just disappointing that a significant number were disallowed due to things like information being wrong. Having automatic updates would be amazing.

A lot of people, as shown by the census, shown by the voting, and shown by the special votes, move around within a three-year period. Lots of people, especially in Auckland- Yes renters, et cetera, a lot of moving around. The floods displaced hundreds and hundreds of families. And so the more we can make it easier for people, because they're not thinking about it every day, "Oh, I need to update my enrollment,"

until they get there and go, "Why can't I vote?" Or, "Why didn't I get my voting papers?" Or, "Why did someone vote for me when I didn't vote?" Right. Thank you, Mr. Hills, for a comprehensive submission this morning and coming from Auckland. Right. Thank you very much. We've got Kay Jones, who's joining us, becoming a regular, which is nice. Right. We've got five minutes with you.

Kia ora, can I say lovely to see you all, and there's some new faces I haven't met before. I would just endorse many of the comments made by local government in New Zealand. Richard Hills, I've been watching online, the previous ones. And I'd also, even more strongly than in my written submission, support the move away from postal voting and to bring back a consistent in-person vote like we have for central parliamentary elections.

I don't want to talk too long because you may have questions, but an example would be of community education. So a friend who's a Muslim woman in an area not Wellington, organized with her community education about what voting rights are here, because this is something that some of the members of the group did not have in their countries of origin.

They are now New Zealand permanent residents and citizens, but voting is new. So educating them about parliamentary elections and that system was a challenge, but they got through it. They understood it. When it came to then saying, "Oh, by the way, we also have local government elections, which use a different process"- No... it was a really difficult education issue. And unless they actually, by that stage, knew a candidate who was standing, they were not engagedWith the process of posting. For some of them, they didn't have

security of housing, so the mail was difficult. There were a lot of things. So that's just one group. But I'm also a community moderator and in the online group of over 2,000 people that I moderate, every week there is somebody concerned about a parcel missing or a parcel that's been misdelivered to them.

And that's parcels, so we know about them, but people don't know what mail they don't get except when there are reports of, "Oh, if you're in that block of flats, all your ballot papers have been strewn down the street." And yes, we had those reports. And yes, we can tell people they can get a special vote if they know in advance, but for many of them, the details they get does not make it easy, and so they just don't vote. So there's a whole bunch of people who don't get their right to vote because of procedural issues, as opposed to the much easier

process, which is still not perfect, but easier for being able to go to a voting booth and cast your ballot. So I'll leave it to questions. Thank you very much. Right. Questions, members? Okay. No. Good. One. Since there's no question, I mean, no one else. Oh, thank you very much.

I think the burning question, we've heard so much about postal votes and things like that contributing to low voter turnout. Low turn. But it's very low. It's very low in Auckland. And we just heard one of the councilors go on about so many other things, but I look at people like him, I'm like, "Why is it so low in Auckland?"

And since we've got a bit of time. Well, I've lived in Auckland. And while, because as you've gathered, I'm a chatty person who goes and talks with neighbors and sort of other people and gets engaged in communities, but that's not the case for everyone. There are many communities within Auckland who tend to operate within themselves, and unless they have a point of engagement of somebody coming to them or being part of their community, they don't see the relevance to them.

There isn't sort of a big publicity campaign to raise the awareness of what local government does and how it connects to them and why their votes make a difference. We get far more information now, definitely echo those calls for having the Electoral Commission taking a stronger role so that there can be more work together with community groups about raising the awareness of what the importance of their vote means and why they should have an opportunity and, of course, making sure it's simplified and modern. So like a special voting paper that had lists

occupation for somebody who's like, "Am I a contractor? Am I whatever?" All the barriers need-... to be removed to make it as simple as possible, and it needs to be done in advance, not just in the last two weeks before an election, because it takes people a while to get their head around ideas. And even things like, yes, Auckland Council, Wellington Council, many councils have their meetings online, but people don't necessarily go to them and know about them.

And you're going to be hearing after me from Sam Summers and people on the It's in the Ballot, and they do a great job of trying to make it lively and engaging for what the voting is. But they're not able to do that thing beforehand to say, "Oh, this is really important." You get really good turnouts at local government events if there is a particular issue of importance- Mm... whether it's a polluted river or whether it's dog attacks in an area.

But that's- Right... a single incident type of thing, and people don't always remember that when it comes round to the how can they actually make sure that people who've got good ideas get on the council and can engage with them. Okay. You know. All right. Hey, thank you, Kay, for coming again, for your submission.

Well, I'm actually going to go through a little bit more, so hope there might be time for questions. There might not be. So, Tina Koto Katua. I'm Charlotte. I'm a district counselor in Whanganui. So I am going to be speaking from my direct experience of the 2025 elections in our district, and also in support of my council's formal submission.

So central to my point today is our local election system is no longer aligned with the realities of how people live, move, and engage with democracy. The 2025 elections exposed major weaknesses in both how voting papers reach people and how democratic information reaches them. The first is the growing fragility of the postal voting. For many years, postal voting has been practical and trusted, but the conditions that made it effective are changing.

People are moving more frequently between rentals, temporary housing, family homes, and care settings, and housing instability and transience are now a normal reality for many New Zealanders. At the same time, the postal system itself is becoming less central to everyday life. Many people, particularly younger voters, simply do not interact with their letterboxes in the same way previous generations did.During the campaign period, I personally walked past many letterboxes containing damaged and uncollected voting papers. That image has stayed with me

because it symbolizes a voting system that is becoming increasingly disconnected from the realities of modern life. And the impact of this was visible in the growing reliance on special votes. In Wanganui, around 18% of special votes were disallowed. Approximately 500 people who believed they had successfully voted, but whose votes were never counted.

And I want to be very clear that 500 people thinking they participated in local democracy only to have their vote excluded is not a low number. It's a warning sign. When some districts across the country reported disallowance rates about as high as 60%, this points clearly to a structural national issue, not an isolated local error. The second issue I want to emphasize is the omission of Maori Ward candidate information that was seen in multiple districts, including my own.

Mm. The issue is not simply the absence of profiles for individual candidates. The deeper harm is that many candidate statements do more than just describe the person standing. They help voters understand the broader purpose and democratic value of dedicated Maori representation. So when those profiles were omitted, voters received less civic information about the value of Maori representation itself.

That is particularly serious where voters were simultaneously being asked to vote in referenda on whether Maori wards should continue. So in effect, some voters were asked to decide the future of Maori representation while being given less information about that representation than they received for general ward positions.

That creates an inequitable information environment, and it risks shaping referendum outcomes themselves. In Wanganui, the omission also exposed an accountability gap, because the election was being delivered through a private contractual agreement. There was no sufficiently clear obligation on the provider to own and rapidly correct the error.

Mm. For something as fundamental as democratic participation, accountability should never depend on the fine print of a contract. For me, these issues point to the same conclusion: incremental delivery fixes are no longer enough. Orange bins, DX Mail, and other mitigations may improve convenience, but they do not address the deeper structural weaknesses now visible in local election delivery.

We need nationally consistent standards for voter communication, special votes, candidate information, and election administration. So I support transferring operational responsibility for local elections to the Electoral Commission while preserving a strong local role for councils in voter education, community outreach, and democratic leadership.

The systems should be nationally robust. The relationships must remain local. So in closing, local democracy depends on public trust. If people cannot reliably receive their voting papers, if hundreds of special votes are discarded, and if voters do not receive equitable information about the purpose of representation itself, then confidence in the legitimacy of the result is weakened.

This no longer is a matter of process refinement. It is now a question of democratic resilience. Thank you. Right. Thanks, Councilor. I've got a question from Mr. Carl Bates. Ka pai. Okay. So we'll take our submission as read, rather than going through line by line. Sure. Yeah. And perhaps we can just go straight to our summary because obviously we'd like more time for you guys to ask us questions. So, our summary is on our submission is on page seven. And the first conclusion or recommendation really is the legislative re-review. We would really encourage, if we can, a comprehensive review of our legislation.

Local Electoral Act is 25 years old now. It was written even before the internet, so it does need, we believe, a full review and a full rewrite, particularly if, and it's a big if, we are able to move into some form of electronic voting or something like that, then the whole act, I believe, needs a full reviewThe second point, special vote reform.

One of the points that you have asked us to comment on is the number of disallowed special votes compared to allowed special votes. We believe that whole process needs a review, a rewrite. Special votes play a critical role. We are under quite strict current legislative requirements.

I follow those strict legislative requirements because I must as an electoral officer, and that does mean invalidating a number of special votes, which is really difficult because I'm invalidating them on a technicality. Mm. And someone's gone to a lot of trouble to exercise a special vote, and I as the electoral officer come along and disallow it because they haven't signed it, they haven't dated it, they haven't done it right.

Can you just say what those technicalities are? We've heard about occupation, but- Yes... what specifically? Well, a declaration must accompany a special vote, so it must be signed and dated and witnessed. Mm. And the occupation, of course. One of the biggest issues we had this time around was occupation. We know that the Electoral Commission have now removed occupation from their declarations.

We would like to follow suit and have that removed because it just makes sense. Their rationale is that not everyone has an occupation. What do you do there? So, we just want to have parity with the Electoral Commission and have some of that complexity removed, and a review of that special vote process, so we remove some of the barriers.
Okay, cool.

So that's the... Sorry, I'll just turn the page. Sorry to interrupt you, but- It's all right. No problem. I think postal voting, you will be aware of the limitations of New Zealand Post. We also are aware of the two mail service providers, which you heard earlier on, New Zealand Post and DX Mail. This was the first time that we pretty much split the country in two. So New Zealand Post did about half and DX Mail did about half. And in my view, it worked very well.

We had the longer voting period. We had two mail providers, and they split the country. And as I said, in my view, I think it worked very well. It was also complemented by a significant increase in vote boxes in the community, where people could then hand-deliver it in their places where they go, supermarkets and that sort of stuff, libraries, where they actually visit.

You are aware that Auckland Council is considering going to booth voting. We are doing a lot of work on that. I'm the electoral officer for Auckland Council, doing a lot of work on that. We are talking with them. I don't think it's a silver bullet. There are a number of things that concern me a little bit with moving back to booth voting. The cost increase is going to be significant.

I don't think the voter turnout is going to increase at all. I think, in fact, it may decrease, and I think the resources needed for booth voting is quite significant. And because of that, there's probably a bit of risk in there for local government. But it is permitted under the legislation, and it is a political decision.

Systems and quality assurance, that's with the issue we had with the Poriki District Council, and I put my hand up totally for that one. I'm the final checker of everything that goes out of my office. And I check absolutely everything, and I missed it. So it was as simple as that. We had it on the voting document.

It was not in the candidate profile statement. I didn't see that. The checking process before me didn't see that. However, we've learnt from that, and our software is being amended now so that there is an automatic cross-check. If it's on the voting document, it's in the booklet.

So that's being addressed now. Civic education, that really, really is important. I think we've been pushing for civics now probably for 20 years. I've been running elections for 40 years. We're pushing civics as a national comprehensive program for a number of years, and I think it's really important just to educate electors what does local government do, why is it so important to exercise their vote, et cetera.

In complaint management, there is processes in our legislation now that people can complain and do. To me, there are processes in place where they can go directly to the police if there's an alleged offense. We can submit things to the police directly. So I think that's covered now. I think that really covers our six conclusions and key recommendations.

So, I'm more than happy to take any questions. Right. We've got a number of questions. First one, Mr. Carl Bates. Thanks very much, Chair, and thanks for the work that you do. Yes. No matter who fills or where they are received or who receives them, they all still come back to our staff, so we have a consistent approach- Yeah... of marking those, or of checking those. Yes. where it's through the post. I grew up in a little place called Opua Rahi in Hauraki, and I'm a tangata tiriti of Dutch descent. So, I was very concerned about the local elections for a number of reasons.

One is that my son, who's male, has always come to our place, was dropped off the electoral roll even though he's never changed his address. So I'm just concerned about... He's back on it now. He's 23 years old. I'm just concerned about what's going to happen next election, where now the law has changed and people can't do same-day enrollments or enroll within two weeks of an election.

What's going to happen if they've had paperwork to say they're on the electoral roll and then they turn up and they're told they're not? So, I guess that's a concern that I'm looking to have answered in this process. My other big concern is, although I live in the Whangarei electorate, the electorate next door to me is Kaipara, and I'm aware that there was only 21 votes between the top two mayoral candidates.

But 288 special votes were disallowed. And just listening to the previous conversation perhaps has helped to explain some things. But I just don't really understand how a vote can be accepted as a special vote and then later disallowed because that person was not on the electoral roll or their details weren't up to date. Because I just think to myself, well, if their details weren't up to date or they were not on the roll, why were they allowed to make the special vote?

And yeah. And then when the second candidate, Snow Tani, applied for a recount, the judge disallowed it because they said, "Oh, well, it would be more appropriate to run an inquiry into the wider conduct of the election." But that also wasn't done. So it just feels very much to me like, because I'm aware that there are a large number of voters, particularly Maori, that fell off the roll, and so they were often the ones doing the special votes.

So first Maori were dropped off the roll. Then when they made a special vote, over 60% of them weren't counted. And then when the results were questioned, a judge didn't allow a review because the wrong question was asked. It just feels very much like a deliberate campaign to prevent a Maori candidate from winning in that electorate, which is known for its previous very racist, anti-Maori mayor.

And yeah, so they're just questions I feel that I would like this inquiry to answer. And I just feel that I don't feel satisfied with the results of the election, particularly in the Kaipara area, because I don't feel like they were fair. And lastly, I do support the use of community voting boxes, and I used one myself.
